quizBank.question: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling? When would you choose each?
quizBank.answer: Vertical scaling (scale up): add more CPU/RAM/disk to existing machine. Simple, no distributed system complexity. Limited by hardware ceiling and exponential cost curve. Good for: databases initially, monolithic apps, latency-sensitive workloads. Horizontal scaling (scale out): add more machines. Near-limitless, linear cost, enables zero-downtime. Requires stateless design, load balancing, distributed coordination. Good for: web/app tiers, microservices, stateless compute. In practice: start vertical (simpler), add horizontal as load grows.

quizBank.question: What does it mean for a service to be stateless and why does it matter for scalability?
quizBank.answer: Stateless service: no client-specific state stored in process memory. Each request contains all information needed to process it, or the service fetches state from external store (Redis, DB). Matters for scalability because: any instance can handle any request (no sticky sessions needed); add/remove instances freely; load balance uniformly across all instances; instances can restart without losing state; zero-downtime deployments trivial. Make stateful by externalizing: sessions to Redis, user state to DB, files to S3.

quizBank.question: Walk through the typical database scaling journey for a growing web application.
quizBank.answer: Stage 1: Single DB — works up to ~10K users. Stage 2: Add read replicas — route reads to replicas, writes to primary. Stage 3: Add caching (Redis) — cache hot queries, reduce DB reads by 90%+. Stage 4: Vertical scale primary — larger instance for more write throughput. Stage 5: Functional decomposition — separate DB per service (user DB, order DB). Stage 6: Horizontal sharding — split data across multiple DB instances. Stage 7: Consider NoSQL for specific patterns (Cassandra for time-series, DynamoDB for key-value). Most apps never reach Stage 6.

quizBank.question: What is queue-based load leveling and when should you use it?
quizBank.answer: Queue-based load leveling: place a message queue between a producer (web tier) and consumer (processing tier). During traffic spikes, the queue absorbs excess load while consumers process at a sustainable rate. The queue grows during spikes and drains afterwards. Use when: processing is async (user does not need immediate result); processing is slow (video encoding, email sending); traffic is spiky; downstream system (DB, ML model) has limited throughput. Tradeoff: adds latency; need to monitor queue depth and alert on consumer lag.

quizBank.question: How do read replicas help with scalability and what are their limitations?
quizBank.answer: Read replicas handle read queries, freeing the primary for writes. Scale reads horizontally by adding more replicas. Works well for read-heavy workloads (90% reads). Limitations: replication lag (replica may be seconds behind — stale reads); cannot scale writes (all writes still go to primary); primary becomes write bottleneck; replica is a full copy of data (cannot shard read queries). Application must route queries correctly (writes to primary, reads to replicas). Use a proxy (ProxySQL, RDS Proxy) for transparent routing.

quizBank.question: How do you estimate server capacity requirements for a new system?
quizBank.answer: Back-of-envelope: (1) Traffic: DAU x requests_per_day / 86400 = avg RPS; peak = 3x avg; (2) Server capacity: assume 1000-5000 RPS per app server depending on work; servers = peak_RPS / capacity_per_server; (3) Storage: users x data_per_user for DB size; (4) Bandwidth: peak_RPS x avg_response_size; (5) Cache size: estimate working set (80/20 rule: 20% of data serves 80% of traffic). Add 50% headroom for spikes. Validate with load testing. Revisit quarterly.

quizBank.question: What are the main patterns for scaling a write-heavy workload?
quizBank.answer: Write-heavy scaling patterns: (1) Kafka as write buffer — producers write to Kafka at peak rate, consumers write to DB at sustainable rate; (2) LSM-tree databases — Cassandra, RocksDB optimized for sequential writes (log-structured merge tree); (3) CQRS — separate write and read models, each optimized independently; (4) Batch writes — aggregate many writes, flush periodically (analytics events, metrics); (5) In-memory aggregation — Redis INCR/ZADD for counters/leaderboards, flush to DB periodically; (6) Time-series DB — InfluxDB, TimescaleDB for time-stamped data.

quizBank.question: What is graceful degradation and how do you implement it?
quizBank.answer: Graceful degradation: when components fail, serve a reduced but functional experience instead of a complete outage. Levels: (1) Full functionality; (2) Core features only (disable non-essential); (3) Cached/stale data; (4) Static fallback page with status. Implementation: feature flags to disable expensive features under load; circuit breakers to fall back when downstream fails; cache stale data with Stale-while-revalidate; separate critical path from non-critical (recommendations failing should not break checkout). Design fallback for every external dependency.

quizBank.question: How does Netflix scale its video streaming infrastructure?
quizBank.answer: Netflix Open Connect: proprietary CDN with 17K+ servers in ISP data centers globally, serving 95%+ of traffic locally. Content pre-positioned to edge servers predictively (popular titles pushed to ISPs before users request). AWS for origin and personalization. Adaptive bitrate streaming (DASH/HLS): encodes each video into multiple quality levels, client switches based on bandwidth. Stateless microservices on AWS auto-scale by service. Chaos Monkey for resilience testing. EVCache (Memcached cluster) across AZs for session and metadata caching.

quizBank.question: What is the thundering herd problem in the context of application scaling and how do you prevent it?
quizBank.answer: Thundering herd at scale: (1) Deploy triggers: rolling restart of 1000 servers — all have cold caches simultaneously, all miss cache, all hit DB; (2) Cache expiry: popular key expires, thousands of requests simultaneously miss; (3) Auto-scale: new instances added during spike, all cold. Prevention: rolling deploys with gradual traffic ramp-up; cache pre-warming before serving traffic; staggered cache TTLs (add jitter to TTL); probabilistic early cache refresh (XFetch); circuit breaker to limit DB load during stampede; rate-limit cache misses per key.

quizBank.question: How do you design auto-scaling that handles traffic spikes without over-provisioning?
quizBank.answer: Combine reactive + predictive: reactive handles unexpected spikes (scale on CPU > 70% or RPS > threshold); predictive handles known patterns (pre-scale 10 min before daily peak, use scheduled scaling for Black Friday). Metrics: prefer application-level metrics (RPS, queue depth, p99 latency) over CPU alone (CPU can be high during startup or GC). Warm pools: maintain pre-initialized instances ready to serve immediately. Cooldown periods: prevent thrashing (minimum 5 min between scale actions). Target tracking policy: simpler than step scaling, maintains metric at target.

quizBank.question: What is connection pooling and why is it critical for database scalability?
quizBank.answer: DB connections are expensive: each PostgreSQL connection uses 5-10MB memory and requires authentication handshake. 1000 app servers x 10 connections = 10,000 connections (PostgreSQL max ~100 by default). PgBouncer (PostgreSQL) or ProxySQL (MySQL) pool connections: N app connections share M DB connections via transaction-mode pooling. A connection is only held during a transaction (milliseconds), then returned to pool. This enables thousands of app server connections to share dozens of DB connections. Essential before sharding — often extends single-DB scalability significantly.

quizBank.question: How would you scale a social network's news feed from 1M to 100M users?
quizBank.answer: At 1M: Fanout-on-write — when user posts, write to followers' feed tables in DB. Works for small follower counts. At 10M: Move feed storage to Redis sorted sets (post_id as score=timestamp). Still fanout-on-write but in Redis. At 100M: Hybrid approach — push model for regular users (< 1000 followers), pull model for celebrities (compute feed on read by merging celebrity posts with user's social graph). Pre-compute feeds for active users, lazy-compute for inactive. Kafka for async fanout. Separate read/write paths (CQRS).

quizBank.question: How does sharding help with scalability and what are the operational challenges?
quizBank.answer: Sharding splits data across N independent DB instances, each handling 1/N of the data and 1/N of the write load. Enables near-linear write scaling. Operational challenges: (1) Cross-shard queries — JOINs across shards are expensive, must be done in application; (2) Cross-shard transactions — requires saga or 2PC; (3) Hotspots — if shard key is poorly chosen, one shard gets all traffic; (4) Resharding — adding shards requires moving data (use consistent hashing to minimize movement); (5) Global unique IDs — cannot use auto-increment across shards.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between CDN pull and push models?
quizBank.answer: Pull CDN (lazy): first request to edge node misses cache, fetches from origin, caches. Subsequent requests served from edge. Simple to set up, works for large content catalogs where you cannot predict which content will be popular. Most common model (Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront default). Push CDN (eager): content is explicitly uploaded to edge nodes before requests arrive. Used for: predictable content (Netflix pre-positions popular titles), very large files (ensures no origin load during peak), content with known distribution (software releases). Push guarantees edge availability; pull risks origin traffic during cold cache.

quizBank.question: How do you implement a multi-region active-active architecture?
quizBank.answer: Requirements: each region can serve reads AND writes independently. Challenges: write conflicts when two regions write same record simultaneously. Solutions: (1) Partition by geography — EU users' data lives in EU (no conflicts, regulatory compliance); (2) Last-writer-wins with vector clocks (eventual consistency); (3) CRDTs for conflict-free data types; (4) Google Spanner / CockroachDB for strongly consistent global writes (higher latency). Infrastructure: GeoDNS/Anycast routes users to nearest region. Async replication between regions. Cross-region latency: 100-200ms, so synchronous global consistency is expensive.

quizBank.question: How does Instagram handle photo storage and serving at scale?
quizBank.answer: Instagram at scale: 100B+ photos, 2B+ daily active users. Storage: photos uploaded to AWS S3 (cheap, durable, scalable). CDN (Facebook's own CDN): photos served from edge, global PoPs. Metadata (photo ID, user ID, timestamp, filters): PostgreSQL with horizontal sharding. Sharding key: user_id encoded in photo ID (first 13 bits = shard). This co-locates all of a user's photos on one shard. Cassandra for activity feeds and social graph data. Redis for session data and hot counters. Django/Python app tier on AWS auto-scaling.

quizBank.question: What is CQRS and how does it enable scalability?
quizBank.answer: CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation): separate write model (commands) from read model (queries). Write side: normalized data model, handles business logic, writes events to Kafka. Read side: denormalized projections optimized for specific query patterns (stored in Redis, Elasticsearch, PostgreSQL read-optimized tables). Scale independently: if reads are 100x writes, scale read side 100x. Read models can use different DB technologies (Elasticsearch for search, Redis for hot data). Downside: eventual consistency between write and read models, operational complexity.

quizBank.question: How do you perform load testing to validate scalability?
quizBank.answer: Tools: k6, Locust, JMeter, Gatling. Process: (1) Define test scenarios based on real user behavior (not just peak load); (2) Start with baseline test (normal load, verify SLOs met); (3) Load test (gradually increase to 2x expected peak, observe where metrics degrade); (4) Stress test (beyond breaking point to find failure modes); (5) Soak test (sustained load over hours to find memory leaks, connection exhaustion). Monitor during test: p99 latency, error rate, CPU, memory, DB connections, queue depth. Alert on SLO violations. Fix bottlenecks and retest.

quizBank.question: What is the C10K problem and how was it solved?
quizBank.answer: C10K problem (1999, Dan Kegel): how to handle 10,000 concurrent connections on a single server. Traditional thread-per-connection model: 10K threads x 1MB stack = 10GB RAM just for stacks. Solution: event-driven, non-blocking I/O (select/poll/epoll/kqueue). Modern approaches: epoll (Linux) — O(1) notification of ready file descriptors; async/await (Node.js, Python asyncio) — single thread handles thousands of connections; Nginx/HAProxy use event-driven model to handle 10K+ concurrent connections on commodity hardware. Today's challenge is C10M: 10 million concurrent connections.

quizBank.question: How would you design a URL shortener that scales to 10B URLs?
quizBank.answer: Storage: 10B URLs x 500 bytes avg = 5TB. Use distributed DB with sharding (MySQL Vitess or DynamoDB). Short ID: 7 base62 characters = 62^7 = 3.5T unique IDs. ID generation: distributed ID generator (Snowflake) or random with collision check. Hot URLs: cache in Redis (top 1% serve 99% of traffic). CDN: PUT redirect responses (301 permanent or 302 temporary) with Cache-Control headers. Analytics: count clicks async via Kafka, avoid counting on critical read path. Read path: DNS -> CDN -> Redis (cache hit) -> DB (cache miss) -> 301/302 redirect.

quizBank.question: What is the N-tier architecture and how does it relate to scalability?
quizBank.answer: N-tier: separate application into layers (presentation, application/logic, data). Each tier can scale independently and be deployed on separate servers. Classic 3-tier: web tier (Nginx, static files, SSL termination), app tier (business logic, stateless), data tier (DB, cache). Scalability: web tier scales horizontally (add more Nginx instances); app tier scales horizontally (stateless servers behind LB); data tier scales with read replicas, caching, sharding. Separation also enables: different tech stacks per tier, independent deployment, security segmentation.

quizBank.question: How do you handle session data at scale in a stateless architecture?
quizBank.answer: Move sessions from in-process memory to external store. Redis: HSET session:{session_id} user_id 123 expires_at 1704067200. Set TTL on session key. App reads session on each request from Redis (< 1ms overhead). Benefits: any server handles any request, sessions survive server restarts, easy horizontal scaling. Redis Cluster for scaling session store. Session replication factor 2 (in Redis Cluster) for availability. Alternatively: JWT (stateless token) — no server-side session store, but cannot revoke before expiry. Use Redis sessions for apps that need revocation (logout, security events).

quizBank.question: What is a warm pool in auto-scaling and how does it improve scale-out speed?
quizBank.answer: Problem: when auto-scaling triggers, new EC2 instances take 2-5 minutes to boot, install software, and warm up. During a traffic spike, this lag means the spike is partially unserved. Warm pool (AWS EC2 Auto Scaling): maintain a pool of pre-initialized, stopped instances in ready-to-start state. When scaling event occurs, instances start in seconds (not minutes). Instances have already completed slow initialization (software install, cache warming). Warm pool instances are not serving traffic until moved to active. Cost: you pay for stopped instance storage but not compute. Significantly reduces scale-out latency.

quizBank.question: How does Twitter handle the massive fan-out problem for celebrity tweets?
quizBank.answer: Twitter's hybrid push-pull model: for regular users (< 10K followers), tweets are pushed (fan-out on write) to followers' home timeline cache in Redis. For celebrities (> 10K followers): tweets are NOT pre-distributed; instead on read, the home timeline service fetches the user's followed-celebrity tweets in real-time and merges with pre-computed timeline. Lady Gaga with 30M followers would require 30M cache writes per tweet — not feasible. Hybrid: push for regular, pull for celebrities, merge on read. This was Twitter's key architectural evolution.

quizBank.question: How do you implement database connection pooling correctly?
quizBank.answer: PgBouncer configuration: pool_mode=transaction (connection returned after each transaction — best for stateless apps); max_client_conn=10000 (total app connections PgBouncer accepts); default_pool_size=25 (connections to PostgreSQL per database); reserve_pool_size=5 (emergency connections). Do not use session pooling with prepared statements (connections returned mid-session). Avoid pool mode=statement for anything non-trivial. Monitor: pool_size utilization, client wait time. Tune: if clients wait > 50ms, increase pool_size or optimize slow queries.

quizBank.question: What are the key differences between scaling a monolith and scaling microservices?
quizBank.answer: Monolith scaling: scale the entire application vertically or horizontally (run N copies). All components scale together even if only one is the bottleneck. Simpler operationally. Microservices scaling: scale individual services independently based on their specific bottlenecks. Payment service may need 100 instances while config service needs 2. Enables cost optimization. Complexity: service discovery, load balancing, distributed tracing, inter-service failures. Kubernetes makes horizontal scaling of individual services much simpler. The right choice depends on team size, stage, and actual bottlenecks.

quizBank.question: How does DynamoDB auto-scaling work?
quizBank.answer: DynamoDB provisioned capacity: specify RCU (read capacity units) and WCU (write capacity units). Auto-scaling: Application Auto Scaling monitors consumed capacity metrics. Scale up when consumed capacity > 70% of provisioned for 2 consecutive minutes. Scale down when consumed capacity < 30% of provisioned for 15+ minutes. Min/max capacity limits. On-demand mode: no provisioning needed — DynamoDB scales instantly to any traffic level, charges per request. On-demand is 5-7x more expensive per request than optimally provisioned but eliminates capacity planning. Use on-demand for unpredictable workloads, provisioned + auto-scaling for predictable.

quizBank.question: How do you design for data locality to improve scalability?
quizBank.answer: Data locality: keep data physically close to the compute that processes it. Principles: (1) Shard by access pattern — co-locate related data on same node (hash tags in Redis, partition key in DynamoDB/Cassandra) to avoid cross-node joins; (2) Geographic locality — store EU user data in EU, APAC in APAC (reduces cross-region latency and meets data residency); (3) Temporal locality — recent data on fast storage (SSD), old data on cold storage (HDD/S3); (4) Cache locality — L1/L2 cache in CPU processes same data repeatedly (batching, sorted access patterns).

quizBank.question: What is the difference between throughput and latency and how do they trade off?
quizBank.answer: Throughput: amount of work done per unit time (requests/second, MB/second). Latency: time for a single operation to complete. They often trade off: batching increases throughput but increases latency (wait to fill batch before processing); pipelining increases throughput but each item waits in pipeline; caching reduces latency (cache hits) but may not increase throughput if DB is not the bottleneck. Little's Law: L = lambda * W (avg items in system = arrival rate * avg time in system). To increase throughput: parallelize, pipeline, batch. To decrease latency: cache, prefetch, reduce work per request.

quizBank.question: How would you scale a real-time chat system to 100M concurrent users?
quizBank.answer: Connection layer: WebSocket connections. Stateful — each connection held by a specific server. Scale: 100M connections / 10K connections per server = 10,000 WebSocket servers. Connection routing: consistent hashing of user_id to WebSocket server (user always connects to same server). Message routing: when user A sends to user B, A's server looks up B's server via Redis (user_id -> server_id), sends message via server-to-server connection. For group chats: fan-out via Kafka — message published, all relevant servers consume. Presence (online/offline): Redis sorted set with heartbeat timestamps, gossip for server-level health.

quizBank.question: What is Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) and what metrics can it scale on?
quizBank.answer: HPA automatically scales Pod replicas based on observed metrics. Built-in metrics: CPU utilization (target 70%), memory utilization. Custom metrics via metrics-server or Prometheus Adapter: RPS per pod, queue depth, p99 latency. KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling): scale on external metrics like Kafka consumer lag, SQS queue depth, Redis queue length — scale to zero when no work. HPA behavior: stabilization window prevents thrashing (default 5 min scale-down); scale-up immediately on metric breach; min/max replica bounds. Combine with cluster auto-scaler to add/remove nodes when pod capacity insufficient.

interviewTips: Always start by clarifying functional and non-functional requirements
Never jump to a solution — walk through your thought process step by step
Mention trade-offs explicitly: consistency vs availability, latency vs throughput
Draw diagrams! Use boxes for services, cylinders for databases, arrows for data flow
Start with a simple design, then scale it — don't over-engineer from the start
Always discuss failure modes: what happens when X goes down?
Mention real-world systems: 'Netflix uses X for this' shows depth
Back-of-envelope calculations impress interviewers — estimate QPS, storage, bandwidth
Discuss monitoring and observability — how would you know if something is wrong?
End with a summary of key decisions and their trade-offs
commonMistakes: Not understanding the fundamentals of Scalability
Jumping to implementation without clarifying requirements
Ignoring edge cases and error handling
Not discussing trade-offs between different approaches
Memorizing solutions instead of understanding patterns

cheatSheet:
# System Design Case Studies

## Interview Framework

```
1. Clarify requirements (5 min)
   - Functional: what features?
   - Non-functional: scale, latency, consistency, availability
   - Constraints: read-heavy vs write-heavy, DAU, QPS, storage

2. Estimate scale (5 min)
   - DAU, QPS (read/write), storage, bandwidth

3. High-level design (10 min)
   - APIs, data model, core components

4. Deep dive (20 min)
   - Bottlenecks, trade-offs, failure scenarios

5. Wrap-up (5 min)
   - Monitoring, scaling, future improvements
```

## URL Shortener (bit.ly)

**Requirements**: Shorten URLs, redirect, 100M URLs/day writes, 10:1 read/write ratio.

**Estimation**:
- Write QPS: 100M / 86400 = ~1,160/sec
- Read QPS: ~11,600/sec
- Storage: 100M/day x 500 bytes x 5 years = ~90 TB

**Short code generation**:
```python
# Option 1: Hash-based (MD5/SHA256, take first 7 chars)
import hashlib
def shorten(long_url):
    return hashlib.md5(long_url.encode()).hexdigest()[:7]
# Problem: hash collisions

# Option 2: Base62 encoding of unique ID (preferred)
import string
BASE62 = string.ascii_letters + string.digits  # 62 chars

def encode_base62(num):
    result = []
    while num > 0:
        result.append(BASE62[num % 62])
        num //= 62
    return ''.join(reversed(result))

# ID generation: distributed counter (Snowflake ID) or DB auto-increment
# 7 chars in base62 = 62^7 = 3.5 trillion URLs
```

**301 vs 302 redirect**:
- 301 (Permanent): Browser caches redirect. Reduces server load. Use when you don't need analytics per-visit.
- 302 (Temporary): Browser re-requests every time. Server sees each request. Use for analytics tracking.

**Architecture**:
```mermaid
graph LR
    U[User] --> LB[Load Balancer]
    LB --> WS[Web Servers]
    WS --> Cache[Redis Cache]
    WS --> DB[(Database\nCassandra / MySQL)]
    Cache -->|Cache miss| DB
```

Cassandra schema:
```sql
CREATE TABLE urls (
    short_code TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    long_url    TEXT,
    user_id     UUID,
    created_at  TIMESTAMP
);
```

## Chat System (WhatsApp/Slack)

**Requirements**: 1:1 and group messaging, message persistence, online/offline status, 500M DAU.

**Key decisions**:
- **WebSocket** for real-time message delivery (persistent connection per client)
- **Message storage**: Cassandra (high write throughput, time-ordered)
- **Message ID**: Snowflake (time-sortable, unique across nodes)

**Cassandra schema**:
```sql
CREATE TABLE messages (
    conversation_id UUID,
    message_id      BIGINT,     -- Snowflake ID (time-ordered)
    sender_id       UUID,
    content         TEXT,
    created_at      TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (conversation_id, message_id)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (message_id DESC);
```

**Online presence**:
- Client sends heartbeat every 5 seconds
- If no heartbeat for 10 seconds, mark offline
- Store status in Redis (low latency reads)

**Message delivery flow**:
```
Sender -> Chat Server A -> Message Queue (Kafka)
Message Queue -> Chat Server B (recipient's server) -> WebSocket -> Recipient
Message Queue -> Storage Service -> Cassandra (persist)
```

## News Feed (Twitter/Instagram)

**Two approaches**:

### Fan-out on Write (Push)
- When user posts, write to all followers' feed caches immediately
- Read is O(1) — just read pre-computed feed
- Problem: Celebrity problem (Justin Bieber has 100M followers — 100M writes per tweet)

### Fan-out on Read (Pull)
- Feed is computed at read time by fetching posts from followed users
- No write amplification
- Problem: Read is expensive O(N) where N = number of followed users

### Hybrid (Twitter's approach)
- Regular users: fan-out on write (push to followers' feeds)
- Celebrities (>X followers): fan-out on read (pull when needed)
- User follows celebrity: mark as "pull needed" in their feed

```python
def get_feed(user_id, page_token):
    # Get pre-computed feed (from fan-out-on-write users)
    feed = redis.zrevrange(f'feed:{user_id}', 0, 19)
    
    # Merge with celebrities' recent posts (fan-out-on-read)
    celebrity_ids = get_celebrities_followed(user_id)
    for celeb_id in celebrity_ids:
        recent_posts = db.get_recent_posts(celeb_id, limit=10)
        feed = merge_by_time(feed, recent_posts)
    
    return feed[:20]  # Return top 20
```

## Ride-Sharing System (Uber)

**Core challenges**: Location tracking, driver-rider matching, surge pricing, ETA calculation.

**Location tracking**:
```
- Drivers send GPS location every 4 seconds
- 1M active drivers = 250,000 location updates/sec
- Store in Redis with TTL (driver offline after 30s without update)
- Use geohashing to find nearby drivers
```

**Geohashing**:
Divides world into a grid. Each cell has a string key (precision by length). Nearby locations share long prefixes.

```python
import geohash

# Encode location
gh = geohash.encode(37.7749, -122.4194, precision=6)
# Returns: '9q8yy' (San Francisco area, ~1.2km x 0.6km cell)

# Find nearby drivers: query current geohash and 8 neighbors
neighbors = geohash.neighbors(gh)
all_cells = [gh] + neighbors
drivers = [redis.smembers(f'drivers:{cell}') for cell in all_cells]
```

**Matching algorithm**:
1. Find all available drivers within 2km (geohash query)
2. Filter by: vehicle type, acceptance rate, direction match
3. Select closest driver (Euclidean distance on coordinates)
4. Send request — driver has 30 seconds to accept
5. If rejected, try next closest driver

**Surge pricing**:
- Calculate demand/supply ratio per geohash cell per time window
- If ratio > threshold, apply multiplier
- Multiplier shown to rider before booking

## Design Framework Checklist

```
For every system design:
[ ] What's the scale? (QPS, DAU, data size)
[ ] What are the read/write patterns?
[ ] What consistency is required? (strong vs eventual)
[ ] What availability is required? (99.9% vs 99.999%)
[ ] What are the latency requirements? (p99 < 100ms?)
[ ] Where are the bottlenecks? (DB, network, compute)
[ ] How does it fail? (circuit breakers, retries, DLQ)
[ ] How does it scale? (horizontal, sharding, CDN)
[ ] How do you monitor it? (metrics, alerts, tracing)
```

quizBank.question: How would you generate unique short codes for a URL shortener?
quizBank.answer: Two main approaches: (1) Hash-based: MD5/SHA256 the long URL, take first 7 characters (base62). Problem: hash collisions and same URL produces same code (collision with different long URLs). (2) ID-based (preferred): Generate a unique integer ID (auto-increment DB, or distributed Snowflake ID), then encode in base62. 7 base62 characters = 62^7 = 3.5 trillion unique URLs. Advantages: no collisions, monotonically increasing (cache-friendly), no need to store long-to-short mapping lookup. For distributed ID generation, use Snowflake: 41 bits timestamp + 10 bits machine ID + 12 bits sequence = 64-bit unique ID.

quizBank.question: Should a URL shortener use 301 or 302 redirects?
quizBank.answer: 301 (Moved Permanently): Browser caches the redirect permanently. On subsequent visits, the browser goes directly to the long URL without hitting the shortener server. Pros: reduces server load. Cons: cannot track individual visits (analytics) or change the redirect later without cache invalidation. 302 (Found/Temporary): Browser re-requests the shortener on every visit. The shortener server sees every request. Pros: full analytics (click tracking, user agent, referrer), ability to update the target URL, A/B testing. Cons: server load is higher. Use 302 for analytics-enabled shorteners (bit.ly, tinyurl commercial). Use 301 for simple URL shorteners where analytics are not needed.

quizBank.question: How does WhatsApp handle message delivery when a recipient is offline?
quizBank.answer: When recipient is offline: (1) Sender's chat server sends message to recipient's designated chat server (routed by user ID). (2) Chat server checks Redis — user is offline. (3) Message is stored in Cassandra with status 'pending'. (4) Push notification is sent via APNS (iOS) or FCM (Android) to wake the app. (5) When recipient comes online and opens app, client fetches pending messages from server. (6) Once delivered and acknowledged by client, message status updates to 'delivered'. End-to-end encryption: messages encrypted on sender's device; server stores encrypted ciphertext and cannot read it.

quizBank.question: What is the celebrity problem in news feed systems and how is it solved?
quizBank.answer: The celebrity problem (also called the fan-out problem): If Justin Bieber (100M followers) posts a tweet and you use fan-out-on-write (pre-compute feed), you need to write to 100M users' feed caches per post. At 100M users x 500 bytes = 50GB of writes per tweet, instantly. Solution (Twitter's hybrid approach): (1) Regular users (< threshold followers): fan-out on write — write to followers' feed caches immediately. (2) Celebrities (> threshold): fan-out on read — do not pre-compute. (3) When reading feed: fetch pre-computed posts from regular users + pull latest posts from celebrities you follow, merge by timestamp.

quizBank.question: How does Uber match drivers to riders efficiently?
quizBank.answer: High-level matching flow: (1) Rider requests a ride. (2) System determines rider's geohash cell and queries nearby driver pool (current cell + 8 neighbors). (3) Filters available drivers by vehicle type, acceptance rate, direction. (4) Ranks candidates by estimated pickup time (considers distance and traffic). (5) Sends offer to best driver; driver has 30 seconds to accept. (6) On rejection, offer goes to next candidate. Geohashing: earth divided into a grid where each cell has a string key. Precision 6 = ~1.2km x 0.6km cells. Store active drivers as Redis sets per geohash cell (SADD drivers:9q8yy driver_id). Query 9 cells to get nearby drivers.

quizBank.question: How would you design a notification system for a mobile app with 500M users?
quizBank.answer: Components: (1) Notification Service — receives event (order shipped, like received) from upstream services via Kafka topic. (2) User Preference Service — checks if user has notifications enabled for this type. (3) Throttle/Rate Limiter — prevents spam (max N notifications/hour per user). (4) Fan-out Service — for broadcast notifications, expands to list of target users. (5) Delivery Service — calls APNs (iOS) or FCM (Android) with the notification payload. (6) Delivery Status Tracker — stores sent/delivered/opened status. DLQ for failed deliveries with retry. Key design: async throughout, idempotency on delivery (delivery token prevents duplicate sends).

quizBank.question: How would you design a distributed rate limiter?
quizBank.answer: Requirements: Limit API calls per user/API key across multiple servers. Components: (1) Token bucket in Redis — Lua script for atomic check-and-decrement of tokens per (user_id, window). (2) Sliding window log — store timestamps of recent requests; count in last N seconds. More accurate but higher memory. (3) Fixed window counter — INCR + EXPIRY per (user_id, minute). Simple but has edge case at window boundary (burst allowed at window transition). (4) Sliding window counter — hybrid: fixed window with partial count from previous window. Implementation: Redis Lua script for atomicity; cell headers (X-RateLimit-*); return 429 with Retry-After header on limit hit.

quizBank.question: How would you design Dropbox's file sync system?
quizBank.answer: Key components: (1) Client chunks files into 4MB blocks, computes SHA256 hash per block. (2) Upload Service: client checks which blocks are already uploaded (deduplication by hash). Only uploads new/changed blocks to S3. (3) Metadata Service: stores file tree, block list per file, version history in database. (4) Sync Service: when file changes, publishes event to Kafka. Other clients subscribed to that workspace receive event and download changed blocks. (5) Notification Service: long-polling or WebSocket for real-time sync. Deduplication: if two users upload the same file, only one copy stored in S3 (content-addressable storage by hash).

quizBank.question: How does YouTube handle video upload and transcoding?
quizBank.answer: Upload pipeline: (1) User uploads video to Upload Service via resumable upload (TUS protocol — supports pause/resume). (2) Raw video stored in S3/GCS. (3) Upload Service publishes message to Kafka: video_uploaded event with video_id, storage_path. (4) Transcoding Workers consume event, fetch raw video from storage, transcode into multiple formats and resolutions (360p, 720p, 1080p, 4K) and codecs (H.264, VP9, AV1). (5) Transcoded segments stored in CDN-origin storage. (6) Video Metadata Service marks video as available with all quality options. (7) CDN distributes segments globally. Adaptive bitrate (HLS/DASH): player selects quality based on network bandwidth.

quizBank.question: How would you design a typeahead/autocomplete system for a search bar?
quizBank.answer: Components: (1) Trie data structure stores all search terms. Each node stores a character; path from root to node forms prefix. Each node stores top-K most popular completions for that prefix. (2) Data pipeline: collect search queries, count frequency, top-K selection per prefix. Rebuild trie daily from aggregated data. (3) Trie stored in Redis or a dedicated service; replicated for high read throughput. (4) API: GET /autocomplete?q=uber returns top 10 suggestions for prefix 'uber'. Latency target: < 100ms. Scale: 5B queries/day = 58K QPS; with 10:1 read to write ratio, autocomplete gets ~580K QPS. Optimization: cache hot prefixes at CDN; debounce client requests (only send after 200ms pause in typing).

quizBank.question: What are the key differences between designing a system for 10K vs 10M vs 1B users?
quizBank.answer: 10K users: Single server, simple DB (PostgreSQL), no cache needed, basic monitoring. Vertical scaling works. 10M users: Multiple app servers + load balancer, read replicas, Redis cache (cache hit rate target >90%), CDN for static assets, basic sharding if write-heavy. 1B users: Multi-region active-active, database sharding (application-level or platform like Vitess/TiDB), purpose-built databases (Cassandra for high writes, Elasticsearch for search), sophisticated CDN strategy, circuit breakers, comprehensive observability, on-call rotations. The architecture changes fundamentally at each scale — 10M is not just '1000x 10K'.

quizBank.question: How would you approach designing Google Maps?
quizBank.answer: Core features: routing, search, live traffic. Key components: (1) Map data storage: vector tiles (Google's format) stored in S3, served via CDN. Tiles pre-rendered at multiple zoom levels. (2) Search: inverted index on place names + geospatial index (R-tree or S2 cells) for location queries. (3) Routing: road network as a weighted graph. Dijkstra/A* for routing. Precomputed highway routes with contraction hierarchies for long-distance routing. (4) Live traffic: aggregate GPS data from millions of devices (Kafka stream processing), compute average speed per road segment, update edge weights. (5) ETA: ML model trained on historical traffic patterns, current conditions, and route features. Scale: 1B+ users, map data is petabytes.
